title: podman api cause conmon defunct leak
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**
We are using podman-in-docker as a task runner and found that we can easily exhaust our `pid_max` on the host as containers and exec sessions are cycled through their [short] life. Further investigation highlights that the `conmon` (and `fuse-overlayfs`*) processes are not cleaning up properly from container start and exec sessions (after 5min delay) and instead go into a `defunct` state.

This was successfully reproduced (for the sake of `conmon` leak) with the below script using either 3.4.1 or 4.2.1 podman images.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

*(excuse my rudimentary bash script, it isn't my forte)*
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash

# [re]start podman-in-docker API
docker stop podman 2>> /dev//null

echo "starting podman api"
docker run --privileged -d --rm --name podman -p 2375:2375 quay.io/containers/podman:v4.2.1 podman --log-level info system service tcp:0.0.0.0:2375 -t=0

# wait for podman API to start
echo ""
until curl --output /dev/null --silent --head --fail http://localhost:2375/libpod/_ping; do
    echo -e '\e[1A\e[K'
    echo -n "waiting for podman api"
    sleep 1
done
echo ""

# spin up 5 containers and a exec session for each
for ((i=0;i<5;i++))
do
  container=$(docker --host=localhost:2375 run -d --rm busybox sleep 30)
  docker --host=localhost:2375 exec -d "${container}" sleep 3
done

start="$(date +%s)"
time=0
count=$(($(ps -A | pgrep conmon | wc -l)))

# wait up to 6 minutes for conmon processes count to report zero
echo "conmon: ${count};"
while [ $count -gt 0 ] && [ $time -lt 360 ];
do
  time="$(($(date +%s)-start))"
  echo -e '\e[1A\e[K'

  count=$(($(ps -A | pgrep conmon | wc -l)))
  echo -n "conmon: ${count}; ${time} seconds"
done
echo ""
```

**Describe the results you received:**
`conmon` processes in defunct state after exec sessions end and/or containers themselves stopped/removed, eventually exhausting pids and causing downstream errors in podman and across the host.
from a local (WSLv2) run:
```bash
$ ./conmon-leak.sh
podman
62815be4ed8d72961df9ca97989108ac5e93bdd1853db0577f84e37b7bd1c957

waiting for podman api
Unable to find image 'busybox:latest' locally
22b70bddd3ac: Pulling fs layer
22b70bddd3ac: Download complete
bc01a3326866: Pulling fs layer
bc01a3326866: Download complete
bc01a3326866: Download complete

conmon: 10; 360 seconds

$ ps -A | grep conmon                                                                                                                                                                                             
3478883 ?        00:00:00 conmon <defunct>
3478899 ?        00:00:00 conmon <defunct>
3479082 ?        00:00:00 conmon <defunct>
3479099 ?        00:00:00 conmon <defunct>
3479289 ?        00:00:00 conmon <defunct>
3479306 ?        00:00:00 conmon <defunct>
3479495 ?        00:00:00 conmon <defunct>
3479512 ?        00:00:00 conmon <defunct>
3479698 ?        00:00:00 conmon <defunct>
3479714 ?        00:00:00 conmon <defunct>
```
\* Additionally, in our production (AWS EKS (k8s)) and VSphere CentOS deployments, multiple `fuse-overlayfs` process were in a defunct state as well, see attached [conmon-overlayfs_defunct.txt](https://github.com/containers/podman/files/9952224/conmon-overlayfs_defunct.txt) to get a captured state of our AWS EKS in this instance.

**Describe the results you expected:**
dependent child processes are cleaned up after given exec session (5min) delay and containers ended

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**
```bash
$ docker run --privileged --rm  quay.io/containers/podman:v3.4.1 podman version
Version:      3.4.1
API Version:  3.4.1
Go Version:   go1.16.8
Built:        Wed Oct 20 14:31:56 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

```bash
$ docker run --privileged --rm  quay.io/containers/podman:v4.2.1 podman version
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.2.1
API Version:  4.2.1
Go Version:   go1.18.5
Built:        Wed Sep  7 19:58:19 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info`:**


**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman` or `brew info podman`):**

```bash
$ docker run --privileged --rm  quay.io/containers/podman:v4.2.1 rpm -q podman
podman-4.2.1-2.fc36.x86_64
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**

Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
AWS EKS (k8s), VSphere CentOS VM and WSLv2

comments.body:
This is not a leak. See #15377. Closing as a dupe.

If you are actually running out of PIDs, you can adjust the 5 minute timer via the `exit_command_delay` field in containers.conf; lowering to 5 to 10 seconds will probably resolve your issue (depending on how slow your environment is; a heavily-loaded system or a low-spec edge device might need the higher end, but 5 seconds ought to be fine otherwise).

> This is not a leak. See #15377. Closing as a dupe.
> 
> If you are actually running out of PIDs, you can adjust the 5 minute timer via the `exit_command_delay` field in containers.conf; lowering to 5 to 10 seconds will probably resolve your issue (depending on how slow your environment is; a heavily-loaded system or a low-spec edge device might need the higher end, but 5 seconds ought to be fine otherwise).

@mheon I am aware of that ticket, however even after 6 minutes (as the script demonstrates) or even 19 minutes as of now, they are still around.

```bash
$ date                                                                                                                                                                                                           
Mon Nov  7 07:19:30 AM MST 2022
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
$ docker ps -a | grep podman                                                                                                                                                                                      
927078f346a1   quay.io/containers/podman:v4.2.1                               "podman --log-level …"   19 minutes ago   Up 19 minutes             0.0.0.0:2375->2375/tcp, :::2375->2375/tcp   podman
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
$ ps -eo pid,lstart,cmd | grep conmon                                                                                                                                                                             
4161332 Mon Nov  7 07:00:05 2022 [conmon] <defunct>
4161350 Mon Nov  7 07:00:05 2022 [conmon] <defunct>
4161538 Mon Nov  7 07:00:05 2022 [conmon] <defunct>
4161554 Mon Nov  7 07:00:05 2022 [conmon] <defunct>
4161739 Mon Nov  7 07:00:05 2022 [conmon] <defunct>
4161755 Mon Nov  7 07:00:05 2022 [conmon] <defunct>
4161942 Mon Nov  7 07:00:06 2022 [conmon] <defunct>
4161958 Mon Nov  7 07:00:06 2022 [conmon] <defunct>
4162143 Mon Nov  7 07:00:06 2022 [conmon] <defunct>
4162159 Mon Nov  7 07:00:06 2022 [conmon] <defunct>
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
$ docker --host localhost:2375 ps -a                                                                                                                                                                              
CONTAINER ID   IMAGE     COMMAND   CREATED   STATUS    PORTS     NAMES

```
Have you reproduced anywhere that is not WSL? I wonder if this is something about their init environment - I know there are nonstandard things there.

This originally cropped up in our production deployment on AWS EKS and we were able to reproduce it on our vSphere cluster using CentOS VM with the same k8s application deployed. I have only been using my workstation (/w WSL) to try and isolate the issue further. I can further troubleshoot on the CentOS VM (i.e. use the above script to see its outcome as well) later today, to rule out my WSL from any foul play. I did also notice, just the container run itself causing defunct processes, I added the additional exec to replicate our actual application's behavior.

Our production's `ps` output can be seen here: https://github.com/containers/podman/files/9952224/conmon-overlayfs_defunct.txt (filtered to only show conmon, overlay and podman processes).
Could be as simple as there being no reaper inside the outer container. If you add `--init` to the outer (Docker) container, does that resolve things?

> Could be as simple as there being no reaper inside the outer container. If you add `--init` to the outer (Docker) container, does that resolve things?

I think you'd be right, the processes cleaned up after 5mins in my local WSL environment. I'll have our CentOS environment reconfigured to do the same and report back.

Is this something we missed in the docs or just different between podman vs docker when having podman-in-x?
Didn't seem to be called out in the [image repo](https://quay.io/repository/containers/podman) or [article](https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/podman-inside-container).

Thanks for the help and fast responses.

This is a thing with almost any container: by default, inside the container, you don't have an init - and thus, you have nothing to reap processes that reparent (like Conmon does in order to daemonize). It's a problem with Podman, Docker, and most Kubernetes containers (the only implementation I can think of that doesn't have it would be LXC) - stems from the model of single-service containers, where you run just the app you're containerizing and nothing else. Docker realized this could be a problem early on and added `--init` to inject a super-minimal init binary into the container to reap, if required (Podman also inherited this, with added support for systemd if you want to stick a full-featured init process in).

@mheon We already have a small reaper built into the podman service, https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/servicereaper/service.go
We could add the conmon pids to it to fix it when run as pid 1 in a container.
That seems like a good idea - I don't know how common `podman system service` in a container is, but it does seem like something we ought to support.

We added [`tini` ](https://github.com/krallin/tini) to our custom podman container image and used it as the entrypoint. This removed the need for different container configurations (docker vs k8s, etc.) and we verified that it cleaned up the the defunct `conmon` and `fuse-overlayfs` processes in both environments. We additionally reduced the `exit_command_delay` to help ensure exec session creation rates didn't overwhelm the process count as well.

+1 when run `podman system service` in container:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8017604/202066840-7b71683c-a328-4b05-8794-1ba8da32c3c1.png)

```
docker ps | grep podman
c40ef51eb8a2   quay.io/podman/stable:v4.2.0                                                            "podman system servi…"
```
How to solve this?
You need to run an init inside the container running `podman system service`

> You need to run an init inside the container running `podman system service`

Thx! @mheon 
```version: "3.9"
services:
  podman:
    image: xxxx
    init: true
```
https://docs.docker.com/compose/compose-file/compose-file-v3/#init
https://github.com/krallin/tini
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8017604/202385700-4509c520-2e89-40fd-94a0-e33f9352be61.png)
Root cause: A pid 1 process must reap forked child processes correctly. if not use --init, podman service pid is 1.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Looks like this one was solved.
